observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Accountant (MA) to: 

Prepare the necessary correcting journal entry to fairly present the Depreciation Expense and Accumulated Depreciation of the PPE for the identified CY 2018 to 2023 assets in CY 2023:


Reflect the corrected CY 2022 Depreciation Expense and Accumulated Depreciation for the said assets in the comparative financial statements;

Compute the correct residual value by using the 5% of the cost of the PPEs except for the LRNs of which no residual value shall be recognized; and


Provide depreciation for one month if the PPE is available for use on or before the 15th of the month; otherwise, start depreciation for the succeeding month.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2023, pp. 30-33 | The MA took up the adjusting entry.
observations.reason:
The MA did not restate the CY 2023 financial statements.

The MA submitted a depreciation schedule for CY 2024 for PPE.

observations.recommendation:
We therefore recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the:
Acting MT to regularly prepare and update the certified list of unpaid real property taxes as required by law, and ensure that it is supported by accurate and reconciled records. Additional support or staffing may also be considered to help address the office’s workload.
MA to base the recording of RPT and SET receivables on the 
Acting MT’s certified delinquency report, not solely on the Assessor’s levy data, in compliance with the NGAS Manual; and
Acting MT, MA, and the Municipal Assessor to strengthen coordination and regularly reconcile RPT records to ensure consistency and reliability of reported RPT and SET receivables.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
MA and the MBO to establish a formal and regular reconciliation process of budgetary records, particularly before the preparation and submission of year-end financial reports to ensure consistency and accuracy in the figures reflected in the SCBAA; and
MA to thoroughly review all budget entries and classifications to ensure the accurate presentation of Current and Continuing Appropriations in the Original Budget column of the current year’s SCBAA. This should include the correct ending balances of the previous year’s CO, in accordance with IPSAS 24.

We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the Acting MT to:
Immediately update the cashbooks to reflect accurate and timely financial records in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002 and PD 1445; and
Facilitate the implementation of an efficient record-keeping and reconciliation system to prevent future delays in updating said records.
We also recommended that the LCE direct both the Acting MT and the MA to conduct regular reconciliation of their respective financial records, ensuring that cashbooks and accounting records are aligned and updated in a timely manner.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MA, Acting MT, and other staff responsible to immediately submit the lacking documents and ensure that payments made are properly supported with complete documents in compliance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We therefore recommended that the LCE, being the Chairman of the LSB, to:
Ensure strict compliance with the prescribed timeline under JC No. 1, s. 2017 by convening the LSB earlier in the year, and fostering regular coordination meetings among key members, to facilitate timely review and approval of the SEF budget and avoid delays in the implementation of SEF-funded programs and activities; and
Strengthen collaboration between the DepEd representative and municipal offices—particularly the Budget, Accounting, and the Treasurer’s Offices—to monitor the timely submission of required planning documents such as the SIDP, SEF income statements, and budget ceilings, and to align budget proposals with actual needs and available resources.

We therefore recommend that the LCE instruct the LSB to ensure that the SEF Budget be presented by P/A/P, by allotment class and per school, using SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1 (Annex A) in the ensuing budget years, and see to it that lump-sum appropriations and appropriations with generic descriptions be avoided to promote transparency, accountability, and equitable resource allocation among schools.
We therefore recommended that the LCE, being the Chairman of the LSB, to:
Ensure strict compliance with the prescribed timeline under JC No. 1, s. 2017 by convening the LSB earlier in the year, and fostering regular coordination meetings among key members, to facilitate timely review and approval of the SEF budget and avoid delays in the implementation of SEF-funded programs and activities; and
Strengthen collaboration between the DepEd representative and municipal offices—particularly the Budget, Accounting, and the Treasurer’s Offices—to monitor the timely submission of required planning documents such as the SIDP, SEF income statements, and budget ceilings, and to align budget proposals with actual needs and available resources.

We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the MA to:
Immediately submit the DVs for the transactions totaling ₱1,708,575.60, including all complete and appropriate supporting documents; and
Strengthen coordination between the Accounting Office and other concerned offices involved in SEF transactions to ensure that disbursement documents are timely consolidated, reviewed, and submitted in accordance with the prescribed deadlines under COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MA to:
Conduct a comprehensive review of the compensation tax computations to ensure that all compensation components and employee benefits are accurately classified, recognized and subjected to the appropriate tax treatment in accordance with existing BIR regulations, including the TRAIN Law and relevant revenue issuances;
Implement necessary adjustments and corrections for any misclassified or unreported taxable benefits, including the excess portion of the clothing allowance, to ensure accurate tax reporting and compliance.; and
Establish or update internal guidelines and checklists for tax computation to ensure compliance and consistency in the future application of tax laws on employee compensation and benefits.

We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the DRRMO to:
Assign specific indicative start and completion dates in the LDRRMFIP for each PPA, particularly under the thematic areas of Disaster Prevention and Mitigation and Disaster Preparedness, in accordance with the prescribed template under COA Circular No. 2012-002; and
Conduct internal discussions with concerned offices such as the BAC, Budget, and Accounting, prior to the finalization of the LDRRMFIP to ensure that the scheduled PPAs are realistically implementable within their intended timeframes.

We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to:
Consider conducting competitive bidding for the procurement of meals and snacks by entering into an FA with the winning bidder to streamline and simplify the procurement process, while ensuring compliance with applicable regulations and promoting efficiency, transparency, and cost-effectiveness in the use of public funds; and
Stay updated on procurement laws by attending seminars and training programs related to government procurement.

We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the GAD Focal Person, through the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), to:
Ensure that only feasible and implementation-ready PPAs—with complete requirements and clear responsibilities—are included in the GAD Plan and Budget; and
Coordinate early with concerned offices and ensure that appropriate documentation is secured to support the readiness and implementation of the GAD PPAs.

We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the MENRO to:
Ensure that the training needs of garbage collectors and related personnel are properly identified and prioritized during the planning and budgeting of the SWMF, to support compliance with Section 23 of RA No. 9003 and its IRR on the provision of competency-based training in proper waste handling; and
Coordinate with appropriate agencies, such as the TESDA and the MHO, for the conduct of formal and standardized training programs—such as Garbage Collector NC I—aimed at improving the safety awareness and technical capability of SWM personnel.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MA to make the necessary narrative disclosures as to the amount and nature of the restricted cash balances under Note 4 – Cash and Cash Equivalents in the financial statements to ensure compliance to regulatory and legal requirements, and proper and efficient fund allocation.

2.12. We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a) Properly adjust the final budgets of the affected accounts to determine the
proper amount of savings under MOOE and continuing appropriations
of the incoming year’s financial statements;
b) Thereafter, present properly the changes in the approved budget as
supported by supplemental budget;
c) Strictly prepare and maintain the required RAAOs for each service,
funds, and PPAs; and
d) Conduct periodic reconciliation of working schedules and RAAOs with
the records of the Budget Office.

3.10. We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a) Trace the balances of the CIP – Infrastructure Assets, and Building and
Other Structures account, respectively and thereafter, prepare the
necessary correcting journal entries to arrive at the proper year-end
balances of ₱4,078,778.92 and ₱2,429,915.10, correspondingly; and
b) Ensure the completeness and accuracy of the accounts and conduct
timely reconciliation of records with the Report on Government PPAs
supplied by the Municipal Engineer.

necessary narrative disclosures as to the amount and nature of the restricted
cash balances under Note 4 – Cash and Cash Equivalents in the financial
statements to ensure compliance to regulatory and legal requirements, and
proper and efficient fund allocation.

We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. Adjust the original
budget amounts of
amount of ₱94,140,396.41 as per
AO No. 8 and LSB
Resolution No. 001, the
final budget amounts to
₱140,899,107.21 in

changes of budget of

budgets of the LGU.
b. Trace the details of the
Continuing
Appropriation in the
amount of ₱48,759,588.56 under

column and thereafter,
adjust the amounts

in the SCBAA.

observations.managementAction:
No action has been taken by
the Municipal Accountant in
adjusting its 2020 SCBAA.
The Municipal Accountant
maintained records of the
Continuing Appropriations in
the original budget column
and thereafter, failed to

The CY 2020 SCBAA remained unadjusted due to the fact that the said statement was not presented in comparative amounts.
The Continuing Appropriations in CY 2020 SCBAA were left unadjusted. Furthermore, since the Accounting Office made no adjustment in the said balances, the forwarded balances for CY 2021 did not also coincide with the presented unadjusted SCBAA of the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. Review the list of Accounts
Payable payables as
presented in the subsidiary
ledger and to provide
adjustment in the books of
the LGU after ascertaining
the existence of Accounts
Payable.
b. Trace in his records the
settlement of payees which
the LGU had an outstanding
liability totaling to ₱1,943,938.33 and
provide for an adjustment if
these were in fact already
settled but incorrectly
recognized in the books;
As to overpayment of
₱128,172.08, trace if the
amount was supported with
an establishment of Accounts
Payable and if none, adjust
through recognition of Prior
Period Adjustment provided
that these were covered with
claims.
c. Submit to audit team
copies of documents in
support of the total accounts
payable of the LGU recognized
through allowing the
submission of Disbursement
Vouchers for its settlement.
d. Reclassify the total amount
of reclassified ₱3,088,510.49 from
Accounts Payable to
Guaranty/Security Deposits
Payable and Customers’
Deposits amounting to
₱1,855,322.97 and
₱1,233,187.52, respectively.
observations.managementAction:
The Accounts Payable recognized
in the books is substantiated with
the subsidiary ledgers.
The Accounts Payable account was
adjusted accordingly. The amount
of payables recognized in the
books is substantiated with the
auxiliary and subsidiary ledgers.
Although some Accounts Payable
were already settled through the
submission of Disbursement
Vouchers to support its existence,
significant amount of Accounts
Payable established in the years
2017-2020 were still not
supported with documents thereby
affecting the reliability of the

The reclassified amount of

Although some Disbursement Vouchers were already submitted to support its existence, significant accounts payable established in the years 2017-2020 were still not supported with documents.
The newly established Accounts Payable totaling ₱2,450,854.05 this CY 2021 was not substantiated with documents (i.e. procurement documents up to Acceptance and Inspection Report) supporting its existence more so that its recording were specifically presented per supplier or transaction such as “various suppliers/creditors” totaling ₱509,390.44.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct:
a. The Municipal Accountant to
recognize the four completed
PPEs in the CY 2020 books
amounting to ₱3,110,762.13.
Derecognize the entry made in

depreciation of the four
completed projects in CY 2020.
c. The Municipal Engineer, in the
ensuing years, provide copies of
Inspection Reports and
Certificates of Completion in
support of the GPPA Report.

Such PPEs were recognized in

incorrectly recognized in 2021.

prepared by the Municipal

The recording or recognition of depreciation expense was only made in 2021. As mentioned in Observation No. 2, completed PPAs in the GPPA Report for CY 2021 was not recognized in the financial statements due to lack of supporting documents to establish its recognition. The Inspection Reports and Certificates of Completion were not furnished immediately to the Accounting Office for its recording.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct:
a. The Municipal Accountant to
base the remittance of RPT and
SET shares of LGUs from the
recognized Due to LGUs. Prior
to that, the Municipal
Accountant reconcile the
remittances from the Summary
of Collections of the Acting
Municipal Treasurer.
b. The Acting Municipal Treasurer
to furnish the Municipal
Accountant the Abstract of the
Acting Collections monthly.
c. The Acting Municipal Treasurer
to deposit her collections
regularly.
d. The Municipal Accountant to
trace his records the difference
between the recognized
collections in the RPT and SET
and adjust the accounts
accordingly.
observations.managementAction:
Due to LGUs account pertaining
to RPT and SET shares are
recorded based on the Report of
Collections and Deposits (RCD) by
the Acting Municipal Treasurer.
Abstract of Collections is being
prepared by the Acting Municipal
Account. Deposits are made
regularly.
The Municipal Accountant
records the collections or Due to
LGUs based on the transmitted
RCD while the amount remitted
by the Acting Municipal Treasurer
is based on her own monitoring
report without referencing it to
the previously recognized Due to
LGUs.
observations.reason: The difference in RPT and SET shares were not yet fully addressed. The Acting Municipal Treasurer's Abstract of Collections was not prepared monthly preventing the Municipal Accountant to reconcile its records to the Abstract. Delays were prevalent in the deposit of collections this CY 2021. The Municipal Accountant has yet to establish and trace the difference between the collections in the RPT and SET, thus, no adjustment was effected.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct the
GAD Focal Person and MPDC to
strengthen the gathering and
collating mechanism of the
Municipality’s GAD database and
CBMS database to ensure
updated, operational and reliable
information, which will serve the
LGU in attaining an evidence-based
planning, policy-making, and relief
distribution in the future; and
augment the number of employees
responsible for the maintenance
and development of the
databases.
We also recommended the MPDC
to coordinate with other
government data-gathering
agencies and enter into a
partnership or an agreement, if
necessary, to facilitate the sharing
of data and information.
observations.managementAction:
The management is exerting
efforts to strengthen data
gathering and collating PPAs
included in the GAD Plan for CY
2021 were not in relation to
women empowerment and gender
equality. Coordination with other
government agencies has yet to be
taken into consideration in the
ensuing years.
observations.reason: As discussed in Observation No. 11, PPAs included in the GAD Plan for CY 2021 were not in relation to women empowerment and gender equality. The Municipality has not prioritized coordination with other government agencies as regards to the strengthening of their data-gathering.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the RPT
and SET shares were remitted to
the Provincial Government and
barangays monthly and in
quarterly basis.
observations.managementAction:
RPT and SET shares were
remitted to the Provincial
Government and barangays.
observations.reason: Delays in the remittance of RPT and SET shares were again noted this CY 2021. As of December 31, 2021, unremitted shares to the Provincial Government amounted to ₱561,952.07 and ₱1,799,484.45 to barangays. Other shares recognized as Due to LGUs such as CTC and Tapuakan shares amounting to ₱410,000.76 and ₱24,562.00, respectively were likewise not yet remitted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal DRRM Officer and the
Social Welfare Officer to revisit
the existing plan for the

relief goods and craft agreements
with other suppliers that could
timely cater the needs of the

calamities.

The LGU holds the same
memorandum of agreements with
the suppliers. Evaluation and
assessment on the performance of
the suppliers was not fully
employed by the LGU. The
ongoing agreements with suppliers
are yet to be reviewed.
observations.reason: The agreements with suppliers are yet to be reviewed. The Municipality has not fully implemented the performance evaluation of suppliers.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Engineer to:
a. Ascertain the actual
completion of the five projects by
showing records of actual
inspection conducted such as geo-
tagged photos and revised
accordingly, the actual
completion date as a result of
validation;
b. Compute liquidated damages,
if warranted;
c. Submit Inspection Reports of
PPAs reported in the GPPA
Report; d. Submit Notice of Extension of
Contracts and its supporting
documents.
observations.managementAction:
No action taken by the
management. The Municipal
Engineer has not acted on the
audit recommendation.
There was no action taken by the
Municipal Engineer. Inspection
Reports and Certificates of
Completion were prepared by the
Municipal Engineer being
furnished to the Accounting Office
as attachment to payments of
projects. The GPPA Report being
furnished to the Accounting Office
was not supported with Inspection
Reports and Certificates of
Completion.
observations.reason: The Municipal Engineer has not acted in the validation of actual completion dates of the five projects; hence, computation of liquidated damages cannot be made. The submission of Suspension and resumption orders, if any, were not provided to the audit team within the reglementary period.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Building Official to
provide status on those issued
building permits without occupancy
permits and impose necessary fees
for completed projects, and
thereafter, monitor those issued
with building permits to secure
occupancy permits first before
actually occupying the structures
constructed; b. Tap the Barangay Officials to
report to the Building Official
monthly/quarterly the ongoing
construction and completed
structures in their barangays in
order to facilitate the Building
Official in monitoring compliance
of the structures with the National
Building Code of the Philippines
and maximize income generated
therewith; c. Enjoin the Municipal Engineer
to religiously monitor newly
constructed buildings, whether
commercial or residential; and
d. Direct the Municipal Assessor to
verify whether real properties
within the municipality are
properly listed in the assessment
rolls specifically those newly
constructed or renovated.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Engineer has not
acted on the audit recommendation
but ensured that building and
occupancy permits were issued in
accordance with existing
regulations.
Monitoring was being conducted.
Verification was not fully
completed by the Municipal
Assessor.
observations.reason: The Municipal Engineer has not conducted review of building and records and ocular inspections that could justify collection of fees. The Municipal Assessor has not provided a status report to the audit team to assess actual actions. Monitoring is dependent on the requests of clients for the issuance of occupancy permits. The Office lacks manpower to fully implement the recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that
Management:
a. Instruct the Municipal
Accountant and the Property
Custodian to continuously
reconcile the balances of the PPE
accounts to identify the details of
the time and unreconciled
discrepancies, prepare working
papers as justification for any
adjustments.
b. Reorganize and reactivate
Physical Inventory Committee and
direct them to conduct periodic
physical count of PPE; prepare
working and submit the RPCPPE in
prescribed format, by account type
with corresponding amounts to
facilitate reconciliation of the
balances.
c. Gather supporting documents
and reconcile the Accounting and
GSO records with the Assessor’s
Office for Land and Building
accounts.
d. Identify unserviceable
properties and submit an Inventory
and Inspection Report to facilitate
for proper disposal.
observations.managementAction:
Partial reconciliation was made
but failed to account in full since
the work demands of the demand
lots of time and effort.
Reorganization or reactivation of
Physical Inventory Committee was
not prioritized. The RPCPPE was
not prepared.
The process of gathering supporting
documents is still on-going. The
GSO and Accounting records were
not yet reconciled with the
Assessor's Office for Land and
Building accounts.
Unserviceable properties were
reported and inspection for their
disposal was requested but not
fully conducted.
observations.reason: The audit team was not furnished with the results of reconciliation, if conducted. The LGU was not able to conduct physical inventory count of properties for CY 2021 hence, the RPCPPE was not prepared. Documents, if there were any, were not sufficient to establish the Land and Building accounts since records of the concerned offices were not yet reconciled. As a result of non-conduct of inventory, unserviceable properties were not fully accounted resulting likewise in non-preparation of list of unserviceable properties.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we
have recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct the
Acting Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Cause the immediate
remittance of collections not yet
turned-over to her and for the
collectors to observe the practice
of remitting all collections daily;
b. Submit monthly and upon
period of January to December,
2019 a consolidated RAAF; and
c. Update her cashbooks daily and
reconcile with records of the
Municipal Accountant.

remitted by the collectors. RAAF
was being prepared by the
Municipal Treasurer’s Office.
Cashbooks were being prepared.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that
the Local Chief Executive:

Accountant and Treasurer to

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that
the documents mentioned which
were considered to be lacking in
line with the discussion of non-
compliance of regulations, be
submitted immediately, otherwise
an explanation be submitted for
non-compliance.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that
the Local Chief Executive direct
the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) and Technical Working
Group (TWG):
a. To include in their post-
qualification a comparative
evaluation of simultaneous or
overlapping bid projects showing
the number of projects with the
same equipment pledged and key
personnel assigned by the same
lowest calculated bidder with
possible effects on the on-going
projects of the bidder.
b. Scrutinize S-Curve, Pert CPM
and Construction methods to
ensure sufficiency and
availability of the equipment
pledged by the contractors
especially with regard to projects
simultaneously awarded to the
same contractors to the same
contractors to avoid delay in
project implementation.
c. To extend the comparison with
other government agencies before
declaring the bids as responsive
especially in validation of the
value of outstanding works of its
on-going contracts.
d. To ensure that at least all on-
going contracts within the LGU
be declared by the bidder to
ensure the NFCC of the bidder.
e. To set limits on the number of
contracts to be handled by key
personnel, in order to ensure
reasonableness of performance
without sacrificing quality and
timeliness of project completion.
observations.managementAction:
Post-qualification evaluation was
conducted and results are provided
in the report.
The BAC TWG reviewed the
documents. The lack of time and
manpower prevented its full
compliance.
There was limited compliance by
the BAC TWG. The list of on-going
projects of the contractor was not
always provided. The key personnel
of contractors remain the same for
every contract. The BAC TWG
failed to include sufficient
comparative evaluative steps.
observations.reason: At least 11 projects had same starting dates and the post qualification evaluation failed to show comparative evaluation of availability of equipment and personnel. The BAC TWG was not able to include in their evaluation the projects contracted within the LGU. The lack of time and manpower prevented full compliance.

observations.recommendation:
In view thereof, we have
recommended that supporting
the suspension and resumption
orders be provided with the audit
team within five days upon its
perfection. Further, we recommended
that the Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Engineer to submit the
order extending the contract time
and the documents to substantiate
the reasons therefore including
those of the suspension orders.
We further recommended that in
the absence of such documents,
the Municipal Engineer be directed
to compute Liquidated Damages
and require the concerned
contractors to pay the same to the
LGU.
observations.managementAction:
All documents supporting the
completion of projects were duly
attached to the Disbursement
Vouchers. The Municipal Engineer
has yet to submit the documents
extending the contract time and
supporting the reasons therefore.
Computation of Liquidated Damages
was not made due to non-submission
of suspension/resumption orders.
observations.reason: Suspension and resumption orders, if any, were not provided to the audit team within the reglementary period. The Municipal Engineer has not complied with submission of documents. Computation of Liquidated Damages was not necessary due to lack of supporting documents for suspension of works and extension of contracts.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we
have recommended that
Management:
a. Strictly implement and
monitor “No Segregation, No
Collection” policy. The LGU to
partner with barangay personnel
to monitor every household; and
b. Fully utilize the MRFs and
strictly follow the implementation
schedule of garbage collection with
regard to the biodegradable, non-
biodegradable and recyclables
likewise be strictly implemented;
c. Impose penalty to violators to
gather income to support the
projects and activities related to
solid waste management.
observations.managementAction:
The LGU exerted efforts in the
implementation of the said policy.
Wastes were being collected
despite non-segregation. The
implementation was directed to
barangay officials. MRFs were not
fully utilized in some barangays.
The Municipality has yet to enforce
penalties.
observations.reason: MRFs were not utilized as intended. The Municipality has not enforced penalties and lacks full implementation of segregation-at-source policies.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that
management to design control
mechanisms to address the cause
of the delay in the submission of
transaction documents and comply
with the pertinent provisions of
COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and
2009-006 dated February 12, 2009
and September 15, 2009,
respectively, to avoid administrative
disciplinary action and
disallowance of claim under
examination as provided for in
Section 39 of PD 1445.
observations.managementAction:
The LGU was able to submit
reports and disbursement vouchers
and their supporting supporting
documents and official receipts to
the audit team. Contracts, Purchase
Orders and their supporting
documents were not being
submitted. Disbursement Vouchers
(DVs) of some paid infrastructure
projects remained not submitted
and there were noted delays in
submission.
observations.reason: The Municipality did submit many documents but some supporting documents, Contracts, Purchase Orders and their supporting documents were not submitted. Significant delays were noted especially with infrastructure project DVs; only some were submitted months after payment, preventing timely audit review.

observations.recommendation:
1.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:

a. Account financial assistance from NGAs in the Trust Fund books.
Moreover, to recognize Subsidy from NGAs account in the books after
the fulfillment of the conditions or purpose stipulated in the agreement
as required under IPSAS 23-Revenue from Non-exchange
Transactions; and

b. Restate the Comparative Statement of Financial Performance of the
incoming year to reflect the recognition of the financial assistance as
revenue.

2.5. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to properly recognize the completed infrastructure assets in
the books of accounts as of year-end in compliance with the provisions of
IPSAS 17-Property, Plant, and Equipment. Moreover, to adjust the
affected accounts with the following entries:

Debit: CIP – Infrastructure Assets 4,209,669.18
Credit: Accounts Payable 4,209,669.18
To record remaining amount of the completed project cost as of
year-end.

Debit: Road Networks 7,502,040.97
Debit: Other Infrastructure 7,997,860.38
Credit: Construction in Progress 15,499,901.35

2.6. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Engineer to furnish the Office of the Municipal Accountant with the
Report on GPPA as of year-end to as his basis in completing the PPE
records before finalizing the Financial Statements at year-end.

3.7. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to diligently observe accounting policies for Special Trust
Funds under the provisions of COA Circular 2012-02 and to make the
necessary adjustments in the books of the Trust and General Funds to
arrive at the correct balance of the Trust Liabilities- DRRM account
amounting ₱7,743,022.54.

4.12. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:

a. Adjust the original and final budgets of the Municipality in the
amounts presented herein, in accordance with the authorized
appropriations provided under its appropriation ordinances;

b. Prepare and maintain RAAOs in support of the SCBAA;

c. Cross-reference budgeted amounts against annual and
supplemental budgets of the Municipality enacted through
appropriation ordinances in ascertaining the accuracy of the
SCBAA; and

d. Reconcile the accounting records with the records of the Budget
Office.

4.13. Further, direct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that account titles
are identified in the annual and supplemental budgets of the LGU and
likewise prepare RAAOs in accordance with the prescribed forms, in
support of its SAAOB.

5.17. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:

a. Maintain the appropriate RAAOs to properly monitor its continuing
appropriations;

b. Limit presentation of Continuing Appropriations only to funds
authorized by an Appropriation Ordinance and refrain from including
those classified as Trust Funds. In this regard, remove the items which
were erroneously included in the Continuing Appropriations of CY
2021;

c. Present the Continuing Appropriations based on the nature of service
of the projects. Reclassify those funded by RA 7171 amounting
₱21,588,616.27 in the actual column to Economic Services and those
under 20% Development Fund amount ₱13,763,006.66 to Others
together with their associated final budget amounts; and

d. Adjust the final budget and actual amounts columns of the Continuing
Appropriations for the unrecorded amount of ₱130,793.78 and
₱741,364.01, respectively.

5.18. Moreover, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Planning and Development Officer, together with the Department Heads,
assign AIP reference codes in preparing the LGU’s Annual Investment
Program to give ease in the proper presentation of the Statement of
Comparison of Budgets and Actual Amounts.

7.11. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

a. The Acting Municipal Treasurer who is the acting General Services
Officer to maintain stock cards for procured inventory, and ensure that
RIS and SSMI be utilized to monitor the movement of the same;

b. The Municipal Accountant to adhere to the provisions of the MNGAS
in accounting for inventory by using the Perpetual Inventory Method,
and incidental thereto, maintain ledger cards, and use the RIS and
SSMI to recognize the related expense in the books; and

c. The Management to fill up the Municipal Treasurer I plantilla
immediately so the tasks of the GSO regarding inventory monitoring
may be given more attention. In case of the absence of applicants,
designate or hire personnel to assist the acting GSO in fulfilling her
tasks

8.14. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to:

a. Require the contractors to post securities of the projects undertaken as
mentioned above;

b. Ensure that contract shall not commence, nor payment be made unless
these are complied with by the contractors in the ensuing years; and

c. Make sure that Bidding Documents and standard forms are duly filled-
out to define the specific undertakings of the bidders

9.5. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal DRRM
Officer to limit the allocation and utilization of DRRM funds to those
specifically authorized under NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2013-01 dated March 25, 2013 and other existing laws, rules,
and regulations. We also recommended that the Municipal DRRM Office
budget and charge its general administrative expenses in the General
Public Services of the General Fund.

10.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to

a. Immediately submit the 25 DVs totaling ₱20,880,991.71; and

b. Stop accumulating DVs of each project prior to submission to the audit
team but transmit paid DVs supported with complete documents
within the first 10 days of the ensuing month.

11.9. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person
to:

a. Create a strategic plan and use appropriate tools such as HGDG to
determine and address Municipality's gender issues by including
programs which directly address gender issues in the community, can
be readily implementable, and avoid duplication of PPAs from other
funds; and

b. Strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanism of the LGU
GAD database so gender issues in the Municipality can be more readily
determined and addressed and intensively monitor the implementation
of activities as listed in the GAD Plan.

12.6. In order to fully implement its programs, projects, and activities under the
four thematic areas, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal DRRM Council to develop a strategic plan for the incoming
year by considering the current COVID-19 restrictions in the formulation of
its PPAs. Moreover, for them to assign specific and realistic time frames
and schedules for each of the PPAs.

13.11. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor rescind the contract entered
into with the Service Provider and require payment of liquidated damages
amounting to ₱350,000.00 in accordance with Annex I and Section 3 of
Annex D of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184.

13.12. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. seek legal assistance from the Provincial Legal Office for appropriate
legal actions that could be availed of against the breach;

b. direct the Municipal Assessor to assess and evaluate the deliverables of
the project already received for the possibility of being continued by
other service providers; and

c. instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to procure the remaining
deliverables, if possible, under the provisions of Section V.D.3 – Take
Over Contract of Annex H of the same law in the earliest possible time
to ensure immediate completion of the project.

14.6. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor review the Municipality’s
PPAs in relation to their COVID-19 countermeasures and prioritize the use
of the remaining amount of the BGCM funds of ₱518,297.24 in funding
them to fully optimize the expected benefits from the use of the said grant.

15.14. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Treasurer to:

a. Levy tax based on the declared gross receipts and not on the
geographical location or size of the establishment and thereafter use the
tax rates provided in Municipal Revenue Code;

b. Conduct annual examination on the taxes paid by businesses using the
annual income tax returns and other appropriate records to establish a
more accurate tax assessment and impose penalties and interests in
case of tax deficits;

c. Collect business taxes from outside contractors who engage in projects
within the municipality applying the situs of taxation. In this regard, issue
demand letters from these contractors or require them to pay the accrued
business taxes with the municipal government before being allowed to
participate in the bidding process; and

d. Require from the aforementioned taxpayers to pay the tax deficit of at
least ₱598,661.94.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
adjust the original budget amounts of in the amount of ₱94,140,396.41 as per AO No. 8 and LSB Resolution No. 001, the final budget amounts to ₱140,899,107.21 in consideration of the net changes of budget of ₱46,758,710.80 based on the six supplemental budgets of the LGU.
trace the details of the Continuing Appropriation in the amount of ₱48,759,588.56 under the original budget column and thereafter, adjust the amounts presented in 2020; and
maintain RAAOs in support of the actual amounts in the SCBAA and the reconciliation with the amounts presented in the SFPe.
The Municipal Accountant, during the exit conference, said that he will look into the details of the budgeted amounts of the SCBAA of the LGU. As regards to the remaining difference between the increases of PPE as against the obligated capital expenditures, he explained that those PPEs were those implemented funded from the Supplemental Budgets of the LGU to which he failed to present. Nonetheless, he made sure that a restatement of the 2020 SCBAA shall be made.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Review the list of payables as presented in the subsidiary ledger and to provide adjustment in the books of the LGU after ascertaining the existence of Accounts Payable.
Trace in his records the settlement of payees which the LGU had an outstanding liability totaling to ₱1,943,938.33 and provide for an adjustment if these were in fact already been settled but incorrectly recognized in the books; As to overpayment of ₱128,172.08, trace if the amount was supported with an establishment of Accounts Payable and if none, adjust through recognition of Prior Period Adjustment provided that these were covered with appropriations in CY 2019. Submit Obligation Requests in support with the obligations
Submit to audit team copies of documents in support of the total accounts payable recognized
Reclassify the total amount of ₱3,088,510.49 from Accounts Payable to Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable and Customers’ Deposits amounting to ₱1,855,322.97 and ₱1,233,187.52, respectively.
The Municipal Accountant explained that the difference of accounts payable in the subsidiary ledger against the balance in the financial statements pertains to unsubstantiated long outstanding accounts. He however said that he will revert the said account in the unappropriated surplus upon shown that these are no longer existing, as recommended by the audit team. He signified compliance with the foregoing recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The Municipal Accountant to recognize the four completed PPEs in the CY 2020 books amounting to ₱3,110,762.13. Derecognize the entry made in CY 2021.
The Municipal Accountant to recognize also the related depreciation of the four completed projects in CY 2020.
The Municipal Engineer, in the ensuing years, provide copies of Inspection Reports and Certificates of Completion in support of GPPA Report.
The Municipal Accountant said that adjustment to PPE accounts will be made as recommended. The Municipal Engineer conferred compliance of furnishing copies of Inspection Reports in support of the status of completion of projects presented in the GPPA Report.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The Municipal Accountant to base the remittance of RPT and SET shares of LGUs from the recognized Due to LGUs. Prior to that, the Municipal Accountant reconcile the remittances from the Summary of Collections of the Acting Municipal Treasurer.
The Acting Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant the Abstract of Collections monthly.
The Acting Municipal Treasurer to deposit her collections immediately for the Municipal Accountant to record the collections of the LGU accurately.
The Municipal Accountant to trace his records the difference between the recognized collections in the RPT and SET and adjust the accounts accordingly.
The Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer conveyed that they will reconcile their records prior to remittance of shares to other LGUs. The Acting Municipal Treasurer signified compliance with the immediate deposit of collections and explained that the matter had been strictly monitored by her. As to the inconsistencies of RPT and SET collections, the Municipal Accountant explained that the difference was mainly in the month of September when he recognized RPT which was previously disputed by the taxpayer. He said that he recognized it under the General Fund and recognized Due to Other Funds with respect to the share of the SEF.
The Municipal Accountant was able to show that he was able to recognize Due to Other Funds for SEF. However, it showed that the LGU was not able to recognize Due to LGUs with regard to share of other LGUs.

Management:
Obtain prior authorization of the Sanggunian before acting on behalf of the Municipality especially on fiscal matters involving the acceptance and utilization/distribution of donated funds.
Obtain the necessary documents from the donor agency of the donated PPE as basis in the measurement and recognition of the assets in the books of accounts.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to adhere with the accounting and reporting guidelines of donations required under COA Circular No. 2014-002.
Management assured that cash donations were utilized accordingly to the best interest of the constituents based on the needs of the LGU and barangays. It assured that accounting procedures and reportorial guidelines for such will be observed prospectively. As regards the donations from PGLU, the Municipal Treasurer will coordinate with the Provincial GSO to seek clarification on the transfer of ownership of received PPE and obtain necessary documents to guide Management in the proper recognition in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person and MPDC to strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanism of the Municipality’s GAD database and CBMS database to ensure updated, operational and reliable information, which will serve the LGU in attaining an evidence-based planning, policy-making, and relief distribution in the future; and as data gathering and collating mechanism of the GAD Database and CBMS database is certainly a tedious work, we specifically recommend the Management to augment the number of employees responsible for the maintenance and development of the databases, more specifically to update the data contained therein.
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the MPDC to coordinate with data-gathering government agencies and enter into a partnership or an agreement, if necessary, to facilitate the sharing of data and information.
Management acknowledged the audit observation, while, citing instances beyond its control, particularly the criteria for the grant of SAP which kept on changing during the first weeks of its implementation. Also, there was confusion among LGUs when some NGAs such as the DA, SSS and DOLE also provided financial assistance simultaneously with the DSWD SAP which resulted in duplication of grant. Still, Management committed to continuously do its best to achieve a data-driven governance through updating relevant information in its databases.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to withhold taxes on payments of BAC honoraria and remit the same to the Bureau of Internal Revenue in conformity with National Internal Revenue Code of 1997 and avoid sanctions on non-performance thereof.
The Municipal Accountant took note of the observation and will comply starting 2021 taxable year. The BAC members acknowledged the audit observation and agreed with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to the Province its shares from RPT and SET collections monthly and to the barangays, in quarterly basis.
The Acting Municipal Treasurer explained that the delay in the remittance was inadvertently overlooked due to the pressing concerns of this pandemic. Nonetheless, she assured compliance in ensuing years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal DRRM Officer and the Social Welfare Officer to revisit the existing plan for the procurement and distribution of relief goods and craft agreements with other suppliers that could timely cater the needs of the Municipalities in times of calamities.
Management acknowledged the observation. The MDRRMO and MSWDO will conduct further evaluation on the weaknesses noted in the performance of relief operations, they shall review the LGUs ongoing agreements with suppliers and strategize on how to cater the needs of people such the scenario happens again.

Reclassify the unremitted GSIS premiums to the “Due to GSIS” account and direct the Agency Authorized Officer to facilitate the immediate remittance of GSIS PS & GS balances.
Exert best effort to locate all relevant records and documents to support the amounts booked from previous years, i.e. through available means such as confirmation and inquiry with former Accountants and other concerned officials. Thereafter, if futile, request from the Sangguniang Bayan to revert the same to the unappropriated surplus of the Municipality.

Based on the foregoing, we have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Committtee to come-up with institutionalized common and standard operation of the water systems whether the operation will be awarded or transferred to Barangays or Barangay Water Association.  In the meantime, the municipality shall provide appropriations for the maintenance and operating costs of the projects.
We have also recommended that Management come up with a work plan and system in conducting inspection, monitoring and evaluation of salintubig projects to attain sustainability and potability of the water.

In view of the foregoing observations, we have recommended that the documents mentioned which were considered to be lacking in line with the discussion of non-compliance of regulations, be submitted immediately, otherwise, an explanation be submitted for non-compliance. We further recommend to strictly adhere with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 in the conduct of procurement of the municipality

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and Technical Working Group (TWG):
To include in their post-qualification a comparative evaluation of simultaneous or overlapping bid projects showing the number of projects with the same equipment pledged and key personnel assigned by the same lowest calculated bidder with  possible effects on the on-going projects of the bidder.
Scrutinize S-Curve, Pert-CPM and Construction methods to ensure sufficiency and availability of the equipment pledged by the contractors especially with regard to projects simultaneously awarded to the same contractors to avoid delay in project implementation.
To extend the comparison with other government agencies before declaring the bids as responsive especially in validation of the value of outstanding works of its on-going contracts.
To ensure that at least all on-going contracts within the LGU be declared by the bidder to ensure the NFCC of the bidder.
To set limits on the number of contracts to be handled by key personnel, in order to ensure reasonableness of performance without sacrificing quality and timeliness of project completion.

In view thereof, we have recommended that suspension and resumption orders be provided with the audit team within five days upon its perfection. Further, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to submit the order extending the contract time and the documents to substantiate the reasons therefore including those of the suspension orders.
We further recommended that in the absence of such documents, the Municipal Engineer be directed to compute Liquidated Damages and require the concerned contractors to pay the same to the LGU.

Likewise, review the collectability of RPT and SET Receivables and avail of remedies embodied in COA Circular 2016-005 to reflect the fair presentation the above-cited accounts.

Management may also follow the provisions of COA Circular No. 93-404 and COA Circular No. 97-001, when appropriate or applicable.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Create an Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical inventory of all its properties and thereafter, prepare and submit the RCPPE for adjustment in the agency books or property records, as necessary.
Assign personnel at the Treasury Office to maintain property cards and keep inventory records updated.
Enjoin the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile regularly the property cards maintained at the Treasury Office and ledger cards of Accounting Office to ascertain the existence, validity and accuracy of the PPE accounts.
Direct the Accountant to comply with the disclosure requirements set forth in PPSAS 17.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the bank balance with the General Ledger balance for the period January to December, 2016 to ascertain the accuracy, correctness and reliability of the cash in bank balance of the Municipality’s Financial Statements as of December 31, 2016. Henceforth, we further recommend that monthly BRS be prepared and submitted to the audit team for timely verification.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive enjoin the concerned officials to review and reconcile the Real Property Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable with Deferred Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income; Due to Other Funds with Due from Other Funds; and the Trust Liabilities-Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund account with the unutilized balance reflected in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Utilization Report. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect the necessary adjustments on the accounts affected.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive enjoin the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to ensure that collections are remitted daily by the collectors and to deposit collections intact and daily or not later than the next banking day to safeguard the government funds from possible loss, theft and/or misuse or misappropriation.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to formulate strategies to intensify tax collection due to the municipal government in order to increase the delivery of basic services and facilities and avail of remedies embodied in Section 174 RA 7160.

We therefore recommended that the Local Chief Executive enjoin the Municipal Accountant to record the purchase of supplies and materials as inventory using the perpetual inventory method and for the Physical Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of the Other Supplies and Materials Inventory to ascertain the balance. Thereafter, conduct physical inventory every semester and submit the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period. We recommended further that the Municipal Treasurer prepare and maintain stock cards, and for the Municipal Accountant to maintain Supplies Ledger Cards, and reconcile the balances periodically.
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to record financial transactions as they occur to fairly present the financial statements.

Based on the foregoing observations, we have recommended that the Local Chief Executive enjoin the Municipal Accountant to:
Submit the liquidation reports immediately to ascertain the propriety thereof.
Submit the lacking documents and comply with the documentary requirements under COA Circular No. 2012-001.
We likewise recommended that the Municipal Treasurer accomplish the Purchase Orders properly and furnish these to the office of the Auditor within the prescribed period.

We have recommended that the management prioritize the implementation of an effective and efficient waste disposal facility by at least creating a controlled dump site in preparation to its establishment into sanitary landfill and to adopt and implement any environmentally acceptable/alternative ways to dispose solid wastes.

We have recommended that the Head of the Procuring entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the Project Monitoring Report approved by him and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We have recommended compliance of the Bids and Awards Committee on the provisions of RA 9184 and comply with the documentary requirements of procurement and for the Municipal Accountant to see to it that such are attached to the Disbursement Vouchers.

The Local Chief Executive enjoin the concerned municipal officials to create an Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical inventory of all its properties and thereafter, prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RCPPE) for adjustment in the agency books or property records. We further recommended that an employee assigned at the Treasury Office  maintain property cards and keep inventory records updated; and reconcile regularly the property cards maintained with the ledger cards of the Accounting Office to ascertain the existence, validity and accuracy of the PPE accounts.
